content.text: Props (short for properties) are how React components receive input from their parents. Every piece of data a component needs but does not own — a string, a number, an object, a function, even another React element — arrives as a prop. Props are the fundamental mechanism of React's one-way data flow.

attrs.alt: Parent component passing name, role, isActive, and avatarUrl props to a child UserCard component with highlighted prop values and destructuring in the child signature

content.text: Props are written on the JSX tag, with the same syntax as HTML attributes. Any valid JavaScript value can be passed as a prop value.
String literals do not need curly braces; all other values do. An attribute without a value (e.g.

React collects all props into a single object and passes it as the first argument to a function component. You can either receive the whole object and read from it, or destructure in the function signature.

Destructuring in the signature is preferred because it makes the component's API immediately visible at the top of the file — a reader does not need to scan the function body to know what inputs exist.
Set defaults with JavaScript destructuring default syntax. A default applies whenever the prop is
Passing null does NOT trigger a default — only undefined does. If a caller explicitly passes null={null} it means "no value" and the component receives null, not the default.
React has no enforcement on prop names, but the React ecosystem has settled on consistent conventions:
Boolean props start with is, has, can, or should
Function props start with on, matching DOM convention
Some HTML attributes are renamed in JSX
These are consumed by React, not passed to the component
When a prop name would conflict with a local variable, or when the internal name is more descriptive, rename it during destructuring:
When a component wraps another and should forward most props through, the spread operator avoids listing every prop by name:
Spread with care. Forwarding every prop to a DOM element will produce React warnings when unknown attributes land on HTML (e.g.
). Pick out the props you want and discard the rest.

A component must never modify its own props. Props are owned by the parent; the child is allowed to read them, but not write them. Attempting to mutate a prop is a violation of React's data-flow model and will not trigger a re-render anyway.
If a component needs to change its own data, that data belongs in
, not in props (covered in the State topic).
JSX placed between a component's opening and closing tags becomes the
prop. It is just a prop like any other.
component does not need to know what its children are — it just renders them in the right place. This is how container-style composition works.
Passing JSX as a Prop Value
Any prop can carry JSX — not just
. This allows multiple named content slots:
What Not to Pass as Props
a component needs. Some things do not belong in props:
— if a value changes over time in response to user actions, it belongs in state, not props.
— import them directly from a module; passing them as props just adds noise to every call site.
— if the same prop must thread through five components to reach one leaf, React Context is a better fit.
When a prop is an object, you can destructure it inline — but be cautious with deeply nested patterns as they reduce readability:

Flat props tend to make default values and documentation easier to handle. Passing a full object is useful when the object is opaque (you pass it straight through to a child without reading individual fields).
Documenting Props with PropTypes and TypeScript
In JavaScript projects, PropTypes provides runtime warnings for wrong prop types:
In TypeScript projects, the interface or type annotation replaces PropTypes entirely and catches errors at compile time rather than runtime:
TypeScript is the recommended approach for new React projects. The type annotation serves as live documentation and is checked by editors and CI.
A full component demonstrating the patterns in this topic:
Several patterns for passing and receiving props appear repeatedly across React codebases:
Collect all remaining props after specific ones into a rest object, then spread onto the underlying element:
Do not pass separate display values when they can be derived from a single prop:
Function Props That Mirror DOM Events
When a component exposes custom events, model them after DOM events — accept a function prop named
and pass a synthetic event object or a plain value depending on what's useful:
Vague names like data or info
when the component could plausibly have multiple delete actions.
Props are JSX attributes; all values except string literals need curly braces.
Destructure props in the function signature to make the component API visible.
Set defaults using JavaScript destructuring syntax; only undefined triggers a default.
Use camelCase; prefix booleans with is/has/can; prefix handlers with on.
Props are read-only — a component must never mutate its own props.
children is a prop like any other; named JSX props enable multiple content slots.
